name: Germany
tagline: From the forests beyond the Rhine to the heart of a united Europe.
summary: Germany's history is one of a people long bound by language and culture yet politically fragmented for most of a millennium. From the Germanic tribes who halted Rome at the Rhine, through the loose sprawl of the Holy Roman Empire and the religious rupture of the Reformation, the German lands coalesced only in 1871 under Prussian leadership. The unified nation rose swiftly to industrial and military power, descended into two world wars and, between 1933 and 1945, into the dictatorship of the Third Reich and the genocide of European Jewry. Defeated and divided in 1945, Germany spent four decades split between a democratic west and a communist east before its peaceful reunification in 1990 restored it as a single democratic state at the centre of the European Union.

founding.label: Proclamation of the German Empire
founding.detail: King Wilhelm I of Prussia was proclaimed German Emperor in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles on 18 January 1871, uniting the German states into a single nation under Prussian leadership. The new empire drew on older notions of German statehood reaching back to East Francia, established by the Treaty of Verdun in 843, and the imperial coronation of Otto I in 962 that founded the Holy Roman Empire. After the catastrophe of two world wars and forty years of Cold War division, the modern German nation-state was made whole again through reunification on 3 October 1990, when the German Democratic Republic acceded to the Federal Republic of Germany.

quickFacts.label: Earliest statehood
quickFacts.value: East Francia, established by the Treaty of Verdun (843)

quickFacts.label: Holy Roman Empire
quickFacts.value: Founded by Otto I's coronation in 962; dissolved 6 August 1806

quickFacts.label: National unification
quickFacts.value: German Empire proclaimed at Versailles, 18 January 1871

quickFacts.label: Reunification
quickFacts.value: Federal Republic and GDR reunited on 3 October 1990 (Day of German Unity)

quickFacts.label: Reformation
quickFacts.value: Begun by Luther's Ninety-five Theses, 31 October 1517

quickFacts.label: European Union
quickFacts.value: A founding member of the European communities (1957) and its largest economy

eras.title: Germania, Rome and the Frankish Inheritance
eras.standfirst: Germanic peoples beyond the Rhine resisted Roman conquest, then inherited and reshaped the western Roman world through the Frankish empire of Charlemagne.
body: In antiquity the lands east of the Rhine and north of the Danube lay beyond the effective reach of Rome. In 9 CE an alliance of Germanic tribes led by Arminius, a Cheruscan chieftain who had served in the Roman army, annihilated three legions under Publius Quinctilius Varus in the Battle of the Teutoburg Forest. The defeat shattered Roman ambitions to provincialise Germania Magna and fixed the Rhine and Danube as the empire's lasting northern frontier. For centuries the term Germania denoted a mosaic of tribal peoples rather than any single polity.

As Roman authority in the west collapsed, Germanic confederations such as the Franks, Saxons, Alemanni and Bavarians settled and consolidated power. The Franks proved the most enduring. Under the Carolingian dynasty, and above all under Charlemagne, who was crowned Emperor of the Romans by Pope Leo III on 25 December 800, the Frankish realm expanded to encompass much of western and central Europe and bound the German lands into a Christian imperial order.

Charlemagne's empire did not long survive him intact. By the Treaty of Verdun, concluded in 843, his grandsons partitioned the Frankish realm into three kingdoms. The easternmost portion, East Francia, comprised the German-speaking duchies east of the Rhine and would become the kernel of the medieval Kingdom of Germany and, in time, the Holy Roman Empire.

events.title: Battle of the Teutoburg Forest
events.summary: Germanic tribes under Arminius destroyed three Roman legions commanded by Varus, ending Roman attempts to conquer Germania east of the Rhine and securing the river as a permanent frontier.

events.title: Coronation of Charlemagne
events.summary: Pope Leo III crowned the Frankish king Charlemagne Emperor of the Romans in Rome, reviving the western imperial title and binding the German lands into a renewed Christian empire.

events.title: Treaty of Verdun
events.summary: Charlemagne's grandsons divided the Carolingian empire into three kingdoms. East Francia, the German-speaking eastern share, became the foundation of the future Kingdom of Germany.

figures.name: Arminius
figures.blurb: Trained in the Roman army and granted citizenship, Arminius turned his knowledge against Rome to forge a tribal coalition that destroyed Varus's legions in 9 CE. Later German nationalism mythologised him as 'Hermann', a founding hero, though his historical aims were tribal rather than national.

figures.name: Charlemagne
figures.role: King of the Franks and Emperor
figures.blurb: The greatest of the Carolingian rulers, Charlemagne united much of western Europe under Frankish rule and was crowned emperor in 800. Both German and French traditions claim him; the partition of his empire in 843 set the German lands on their own path.

eras.title: The Holy Roman Empire and the Reformation
eras.standfirst: A sprawling, decentralised empire of princes, free cities and bishoprics gave the German lands their political form, until religious schism plunged them into a generation of war.
body: On 2 February 962 the East Frankish king Otto I was crowned emperor by Pope John XII in Rome, joining the German kingdom with the Kingdom of Italy and founding what became known as the Holy Roman Empire. For more than eight centuries this elective monarchy provided a constitutional roof over a patchwork of hundreds of duchies, principalities, ecclesiastical territories and free imperial cities. Real power lay less with the emperor than with the territorial princes and the prince-electors who chose him, producing a uniquely fragmented yet durable political order at the heart of Europe.

The empire's spiritual unity fractured in the sixteenth century. On 31 October 1517 the Augustinian friar Martin Luther circulated his Ninety-five Theses attacking the sale of indulgences; printed and distributed within weeks, they ignited the Protestant Reformation. Luther's translation of the Bible into German shaped the modern language and gave reform a popular reach. The resulting conflict between Catholic and Protestant territories was contained, for a time, by the Peace of Augsburg of 1555, which let each prince determine the religion of his own lands under the principle cuius regio, eius religio.

That settlement broke down catastrophically in the Thirty Years' War (1618–1648), which began as a religious and constitutional struggle within the empire and drew in the great powers of Europe. Fought largely on German soil, it brought famine, plague and depopulation on a devastating scale. The Peace of Westphalia of 1648 ended the war, confirmed the near-sovereignty of the empire's constituent states and entrenched the German lands' political fragmentation for the next two centuries.

events.title: Otto I crowned emperor
events.summary: Pope John XII crowned Otto I of East Francia emperor in Rome, uniting the German and Italian kingdoms and founding the Holy Roman Empire that would frame German political life for over 800 years.

events.title: Luther's Ninety-five Theses
events.summary: Martin Luther issued his Ninety-five Theses against the sale of indulgences, sparking the Protestant Reformation that split Western Christianity and reshaped German society and language.

events.title: Peace of Augsburg
events.summary: The imperial diet established the first lasting legal coexistence of Lutheranism and Catholicism, letting each prince set his territory's religion under the principle cuius regio, eius religio.

events.title: The Thirty Years' War and the Peace of Westphalia
events.summary: A devastating European war fought largely in Germany ended with the Peace of Westphalia in 1648, which confirmed the autonomy of the empire's states and entrenched German fragmentation.

figures.name: Otto I (Otto the Great)
figures.role: King of Germany and Holy Roman Emperor
figures.blurb: By defeating the Magyars at Lechfeld and securing his coronation in Rome in 962, Otto fused royal and imperial authority and inaugurated the Holy Roman Empire, anchoring the German crown to the imperial title for centuries.

figures.name: Martin Luther
figures.blurb: Luther's challenge to papal authority and his German Bible transformed religion, politics and language across the empire. Excommunicated and outlawed, he survived under princely protection, and Lutheranism became a defining force in German history.

pullquote.text: Here I stand; I can do no other.
pullquote.attribution: Words traditionally attributed to Martin Luther at the Diet of Worms, 1521

eras.title: Prussia, Fragmentation and the Road to Unity
eras.standfirst: The rise of Prussia, the collapse of the old empire under Napoleon and a growing national movement converged in the unification of Germany under Bismarck.
body: After 1648 the German lands remained divided among hundreds of states, but two powers rose to dominate them: Habsburg Austria and an expanding Prussia. Under the Hohenzollern dynasty, and especially Frederick the Great in the eighteenth century, Prussia became a disciplined military and administrative state that contested Austrian primacy in central Europe.

The old order was swept away by revolutionary France. Defeated repeatedly by Napoleon, the empire collapsed when Emperor Francis II abdicated the imperial crown on 6 August 1806, formally dissolving the Holy Roman Empire after more than 840 years. French domination, however, also spread ideas of nationhood and reform. After Napoleon's defeat, the Congress of Vienna in 1815 created a loose German Confederation of sovereign states, frustrating liberals and nationalists whose hopes for a unified, constitutional Germany were crushed when the revolutions of 1848 failed.

Unification was ultimately achieved not by liberal revolution but by Prussian power. As minister-president, Otto von Bismarck pursued unity through 'blood and iron', engineering victories over Denmark (1864), Austria (1866) and France (1870–71). In the wake of the Franco-Prussian War, the German states were bound into a single empire, proclaimed at Versailles on 18 January 1871 with the Prussian king Wilhelm I as German Emperor.

events.title: Dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire
events.summary: Under pressure from Napoleon, Emperor Francis II abdicated the imperial crown and dissolved the Holy Roman Empire, ending the medieval framework of German statehood after more than eight centuries.

events.title: Congress of Vienna and the German Confederation
events.summary: After Napoleon's defeat, the Congress of Vienna created a loose confederation of German states dominated by Austria and Prussia, leaving national unity unfulfilled.

events.title: The Revolutions of 1848
events.summary: Liberal and nationalist uprisings swept the German states, and the Frankfurt Parliament drafted a constitution for a united Germany, but the movement collapsed when the Prussian king refused its imperial crown.

events.title: Proclamation of the German Empire
events.summary: Following victory in the Franco-Prussian War, the German states united as the German Empire, proclaimed in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles with Wilhelm I as Emperor and Bismarck as Chancellor.

figures.name: Otto von Bismarck
figures.role: Minister-President of Prussia and first Chancellor of the German Empire
figures.blurb: The architect of German unification, Bismarck engineered a series of wars and a web of diplomacy that forged the empire in 1871. As chancellor he dominated European politics for two decades and pioneered the first modern welfare-state institutions.

pullquote.text: The great questions of the day will not be settled by speeches and majority decisions but by iron and blood.

eras.title: The German Empire and the First World War
eras.standfirst: A newly unified empire rose to industrial and military pre-eminence, then plunged Europe into the First World War and emerged in defeat and revolution.
body: The German Empire forged in 1871 was a federal monarchy led by Prussia, with an elected Reichstag but real power resting with the emperor and his chancellor. In its first decades the empire industrialised with extraordinary speed, becoming a world leader in steel, chemicals, electrical engineering and science, and a centre of universities, music and scholarship. Bismarck's diplomacy kept the peace in Europe, while his domestic policy combined repression of socialists with the pioneering creation of state social insurance.

After Wilhelm II dismissed Bismarck in 1890, German policy grew more assertive. A naval arms race with Britain, colonial ambitions and a tangle of alliances raised tensions across Europe. When a crisis erupted in the Balkans in 1914, the system of alliances drew the powers into the First World War, with Germany at the centre of the conflict alongside Austria-Hungary.

Four years of attritional war exhausted the empire. As military defeat became certain in the autumn of 1918 and revolution spread through the country, Emperor Wilhelm II abdicated on 9 November 1918 and a republic was proclaimed. Two days later, on 11 November 1918, an armistice ended the fighting. The empire that had lasted barely half a century was gone, and Germany faced the punitive terms imposed at Versailles.

events.title: The Bismarckian empire and industrial rise
events.summary: Under Bismarck's chancellorship the empire industrialised rapidly and introduced the world's first national social insurance, while maintaining a careful balance of power in Europe.

events.title: German colonial expansion
events.summary: Germany acquired colonies in Africa and the Pacific, a latecomer's empire whose rule, including the genocide of the Herero and Nama in South West Africa, left a brutal legacy.

events.title: The First World War
events.summary: Germany fought the First World War as the principal Central Power. Four years of total war devastated a generation and ended in German defeat.

events.title: Abdication of Wilhelm II
events.summary: Amid military collapse and revolution, Emperor Wilhelm II abdicated and a republic was proclaimed in Berlin; the armistice followed two days later, ending the war and the monarchy.

figures.name: Wilhelm II
figures.role: Last German Emperor and King of Prussia
figures.blurb: Impulsive and ambitious, Wilhelm II dismissed Bismarck and steered Germany toward a more aggressive 'world policy'. His reign ended in war, revolution and abdication, closing the chapter of imperial monarchy in Germany.

eras.title: Weimar, the Third Reich and the Second World War
eras.standfirst: Germany's first democracy struggled and fell, giving way to the Nazi dictatorship, the Second World War and the genocide of European Jewry.
body: Out of defeat and revolution emerged the Weimar Republic, Germany's first parliamentary democracy, whose constitution was adopted in 1919. Burdened by the reparations and territorial losses of the Treaty of Versailles, hyperinflation, deep political polarisation and the catastrophe of the Great Depression, the republic enjoyed only brief stability. Extremist movements of left and right gained ground as unemployment soared after 1929, and successive governments increasingly ruled by emergency decree.

On 30 January 1933 President Paul von Hindenburg appointed Adolf Hitler, leader of the National Socialist (Nazi) Party, as chancellor. Within months Hitler exploited the Reichstag fire and the Enabling Act to dismantle democracy, ban opposition, and establish a one-party totalitarian dictatorship, the Third Reich. The regime built a police state, persecuted political opponents, and from the outset subjected Jews and other groups to escalating discrimination, violence and exclusion.

Hitler's drive for territorial expansion led to war. Germany's invasion of Poland on 1 September 1939 began the Second World War in Europe, the deadliest conflict in human history. Under cover of war, the Nazi state carried out the Holocaust, the systematic, industrialised murder of approximately six million European Jews, alongside the killing of Roma, disabled people, Soviet prisoners, political prisoners and others. After years of total war and immense devastation across Europe, Germany surrendered unconditionally on 8 May 1945. The country lay in ruins, its cities destroyed, its dictatorship and crimes laid bare.

events.title: The Weimar Republic and its constitution
events.summary: Germany's first democracy adopted the Weimar Constitution in 1919, establishing a parliamentary republic that would be undermined by economic crisis and political extremism.

events.title: Hitler appointed chancellor
events.summary: President Hindenburg appointed Adolf Hitler chancellor. Within months the Nazis abolished democracy and established a totalitarian one-party dictatorship.

events.title: Invasion of Poland
events.summary: Germany invaded Poland, igniting the Second World War in Europe and beginning six years of conquest, occupation and total war.

events.title: The Holocaust
events.summary: Nazi Germany carried out the systematic, state-organised murder of some six million European Jews, alongside the killing of Roma, disabled people, Soviet prisoners and other victims.

events.title: Unconditional surrender
events.summary: Germany surrendered unconditionally to the Allies, ending the Second World War in Europe and the Nazi regime amid the ruin of the country.

figures.name: Friedrich Ebert
figures.role: First President of the Weimar Republic
figures.blurb: A Social Democrat, Ebert helped guide Germany from monarchy to republic in 1918 and served as its first president. He defended the fragile democracy against uprisings from both extremes amid relentless instability.

pullquote.text: The history of National Socialism is one of the darkest chapters in German history.

eras.title: Division, the Federal Republic and Reunification
eras.standfirst: Occupied and divided after 1945, Germany was split into a democratic west and a communist east before peaceful reunification in 1990 restored a single democratic state.
body: After 1945 Germany was occupied and partitioned among the four Allied powers, and the wartime alliance soon dissolved into Cold War. In 1949 the division was formalised: the western zones became the democratic, market-oriented Federal Republic of Germany, whose Basic Law took effect on 23 May 1949, while the Soviet zone became the German Democratic Republic on 7 October 1949. Under Chancellor Konrad Adenauer and the 'economic miracle', West Germany rebuilt rapidly, integrated into Western Europe and NATO, and became a founding member of the European communities.

Berlin, itself divided, became the sharpest symbol of the split. To halt the flight of its citizens westward, the East German regime began building the Berlin Wall on 13 August 1961, sealing off East from West for nearly three decades. The communist GDR endured as a one-party state under Soviet influence, while West Germany pursued Ostpolitik, a gradual normalisation of relations with the East.

The division ended with startling speed. As reform swept the Eastern Bloc in 1989, mass protests in East Germany forced open the Berlin Wall on 9 November 1989. Within a year, negotiations among the two German states and the four wartime Allies cleared the way for unity. On 3 October 1990 the German Democratic Republic acceded to the Federal Republic, reuniting Germany as a single democratic state, a date now celebrated as the Day of German Unity.

Reunified Germany has since become the most populous member and largest economy of the European Union, a leading force in European integration and a stable parliamentary democracy. It has confronted its twentieth-century history with unusual openness, making remembrance of the crimes of the Nazi era a foundation of its civic identity.

events.title: Founding of the two German states
events.summary: The Basic Law established the Federal Republic of Germany in the west on 23 May 1949; the German Democratic Republic was founded in the Soviet zone on 7 October 1949, formalising the country's division.

events.title: Construction of the Berlin Wall
events.summary: East Germany began building the Berlin Wall to stop its citizens fleeing west, creating the most potent symbol of the Cold War division of Europe.

events.title: Fall of the Berlin Wall
events.summary: Amid mass protests and the collapse of communist rule across Eastern Europe, the Berlin Wall was opened, ending the division of Berlin and clearing the path to reunification.

events.title: German reunification
events.summary: The German Democratic Republic acceded to the Federal Republic, reuniting Germany as a single democratic state, now commemorated annually as the Day of German Unity.

figures.name: Konrad Adenauer
figures.role: First Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany
figures.blurb: Adenauer led West Germany from 1949 to 1963, anchoring it firmly in the democratic West, securing reconciliation with former enemies and overseeing the postwar economic recovery that restored German prosperity and credibility.

figures.name: Helmut Kohl
figures.blurb: Chancellor from 1982 to 1998, Kohl seized the opening created by the fall of the Wall to drive German reunification in 1990 and championed deeper European integration, including the creation of the euro.

pullquote.text: On 3 October 1990 Germany was peacefully reunited.
pullquote.attribution: On the Day of German Unity

figures.name: Otto von Bismarck
figures.role: First Chancellor of the German Empire
figures.blurb: The 'Iron Chancellor' unified Germany under Prussian leadership in 1871 and dominated European diplomacy for nearly two decades, shaping the modern German state.

figures.name: Martin Luther
figures.blurb: His Ninety-five Theses launched the Reformation, splitting Western Christianity, while his German Bible helped forge the modern German language.

figures.name: Konrad Adenauer
figures.role: First Chancellor of the Federal Republic
figures.blurb: Founding statesman of West German democracy, Adenauer rebuilt the country, anchored it in the West and pursued reconciliation with Germany's former enemies.

sources.label: The Holocaust and World War II: Key Dates
Day of German Unity: Facts and History
